refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable a tele-operator to perceive and command equipment state with end-to-end communication latency that does not degrade safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct functional obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined FR isolates the core functional requirement—enabling perception and command with acceptable latency—while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing 'p95 ≤ 150 ms' (an acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement) and focuses on the problem domain: what the system must do for the tele-operator. The phrase 'does not degrade safe remote operation' anchors the requirement to the safety context without embedding implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor-to-display and command-to-actuation) under agreed network conditions. Conduct human-factors validation with tele-operators performing representative tasks (e.g., precision placement, obstacle avoidance) to confirm latency does not introduce unsafe delays or loss of situational awareness. Cross-reference against worksite safety plan thresholds.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state (sensor data) to the tele-operator interface with minimal delay.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator commands to equipment actuators with minimal delay.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall provide tele-operator feedback (visual, auditory, or haptic) sufficient to maintain situational awareness during remote operation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Dual-path communication architecture with primary and fallback channels, each independently meeting latency budget, with automatic failover detection and switching logic
DP2: Adaptive frame-rate and compression pipeline that dynamically adjusts telemetry and video quality based on measured network conditions to maintain latency within budget
DP3: Distributed edge-compute model where state perception and command validation occur locally at the worksite, with operator receiving only high-level summaries and exception alerts

DP4: Deterministic message prioritization and scheduling layer that guarantees operator perception (sensor→display) and command execution (input→actuator) paths meet latency SLA independently of background traffic
DP5: Hybrid synchronous-asynchronous protocol where critical state updates use low-latency synchronous channels (e.g., UDP-based) while non-critical telemetry uses asynchronous queuing

DP6: Predictive state extrapolation at operator console, where equipment state is locally predicted between network updates using physics/kinematic models, reducing perceived latency and masking jitter
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Deterministic message prioritization and scheduling layer that guarantees operator perception (sensor→display) and command execution (input→actuator) paths meet latency SLA independently of background traffic

potential_sources: DP4 assumes existence of a measurable network path with known characteristics; if network topology or QoS mechanisms are not controllable, latency guarantees become probabilistic rather than deterministic—recommend pairing with DP1 (fallback) or DP2 (adaptation) to mitigate

DP4 does not address operator cognitive load or display refresh rates; must be composed with perception design (e.g., DP6 for state extrapolation) to avoid operator confusion from delayed or jittery updates
DP4 is orthogonal to compression/quality adaptation (DP2) and edge compute (DP3); can be combined without logical conflict, but implementation complexity increases if all three are selected
coupling_check.notes: DP4 is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific protocols or hardware) and logically decouples the latency guarantee mechanism from the communication medium. It supports independence by isolating critical-path scheduling from best-effort background traffic. Coupling risk is primarily at the boundary with network infrastructure and operator interface, not within the DP itself.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network path supports QoS tagging or traffic isolation (e.g., VLAN, DiffServ, or dedicated bandwidth). RISK: If network is best-effort only, deterministic guarantees cannot be met; mitigation: validate network capability before design freeze, or adopt DP1/DP2 as fallback.

ASSUMPTION: Operator perception loop (sensor acquisition → network transmission → display rendering) latency is dominated by network transit, not by sensor sampling or display refresh. RISK: If sensor polling or display refresh is slow (e.g., 100+ ms), network optimization alone will not meet 150 ms p95 budget; mitigation: measure end-to-end latency distribution early, identify bottleneck.

ASSUMPTION: Equipment state can be represented in a bounded message size that fits within network MTU without fragmentation. RISK: Large sensor payloads or high-frequency updates may exceed MTU and introduce retransmission delays; mitigation: pair with DP2 (compression) or DP3 (edge filtering).

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits operator to rely on tele-perception with 150 ms latency for safe command execution. RISK: If safety-critical actions require sub-100 ms latency or operator reaction time is not accounted for, acceptance criteria may be insufficient; mitigation: validate with safety engineering and operational procedures.

ASSUMPTION: No single point of failure in the communication stack will cause loss of operator perception or command capability. RISK: DP4 alone does not address redundancy; mitigation: pair with DP1 (dual-path) or design IM layer with failover logic.

ASSUMPTION: Operator interface (console, display, input device) is co-located and does not introduce additional latency. RISK: If operator is remote or uses wireless input, end-to-end latency may exceed budget; mitigation: measure operator-side latency separately and include in budget allocation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable a tele-operator to perceive and command equipment state with end-to-end communication latency (sensor acquisition through network transmission to operator display, and operator input through network transmission to equipment actuator) that does not exceed p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions, while maintaining operator safety and situational awareness through redundancy and fallback mechanisms. RATIONALE: The original FR does not explicitly bound the latency measurement points or account for non-network contributors (sensor, display, operator reaction). Revised FR clarifies scope and enables DP selection to focus on network-layer determinism (DP4) while acknowledging need for complementary DPs (DP1 for redundancy, DP6 for perception masking). RECOMMENDED DP COMPOSITION: DP4 (primary latency guarantee) + DP1 (failover for safety) + DP6 (operator perception smoothing) to balance determinism, safety, and usability.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates deterministic message prioritization, scheduling, and latency-critical path management for operator perception (sensor→display) and command execution (input→actuator) flows, ensuring SLA compliance independent of background traffic

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests sensor data streams; Teleoperation module prioritizes and schedules telemetry for deterministic delivery to operator display within SLA latency bounds

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and input; Teleoperation module prioritizes and schedules command execution paths to actuators with guaranteed latency SLA

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates safety-critical events detected during prioritization/scheduling to Safety Services for override or intervention

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages network transmission of prioritized messages; Teleoperation module controls scheduling discipline to isolate critical paths from background traffic congestion

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of latency-critical operator perception and command execution paths. The DP's core logic (prioritization, scheduling, SLA enforcement) is intrinsic to teleoperation mission. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert escalation and Network Link coordination require external interfaces, but these are clean, well-defined boundaries. No algorithmic leakage.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns safety validation and override logic, not general-purpose message scheduling. Placing deterministic scheduling here would scatter teleoperation latency concerns across two modules. High diffusion: Teleoperation would still need its own scheduling logic for non-safety-critical paths, creating duplication and coupling.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns vehicle state, dynamics, and actuator control. Embedding operator-centric message prioritization and scheduling here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: Teleoperation logic would be scattered across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, complicating latency SLA verification and operator-in-the-loop guarantees.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP4 here provides no architectural clarity or ownership. Extreme diffusion: Scheduling logic would diffuse across all subsystems without clear responsibility boundaries.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of deterministic message prioritization and scheduling because: (1) operator perception (sensor→display) and command execution (input→actuator) are intrinsic teleoperation functions; (2) latency SLA enforcement is a teleoperation quality attribute; (3) the DP's logic (prioritization discipline, scheduling algorithm, traffic isolation) is cohesive and localized to this module; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout) are clean and minimal, indicating low coupling.
containment_risks: Network Link interface may require bidirectional coordination: Teleoperation must control transmission scheduling, but Network Link may impose its own queueing or congestion feedback. Risk: scheduling discipline could be undermined by network-layer buffering. Mitigation: define explicit contract on Network Link interface specifying priority queue semantics and backpressure signaling.

SafetyAlert escalation path: if safety-critical events are detected during scheduling (e.g., command latency exceeds safety threshold), Teleoperation must escalate to Safety Services. Risk: Safety Services may override or delay critical commands, creating secondary latency. Mitigation: pre-define safety override policies and ensure SafetyAlert interface includes latency metadata.

Background traffic isolation: DP4 assumes ability to isolate critical paths from background traffic. Risk: if Network Link or Vehicle modules generate uncontrolled background traffic, isolation may fail. Mitigation: establish traffic shaping contracts with Network Link and Vehicle modules; define reserved bandwidth or priority levels for teleoperation critical paths.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module boundary is well-scoped and measurable against the acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms latency). Sensor→display and input→actuator paths are traceable end-to-end within this module's ownership. Latency instrumentation and SLA monitoring can be localized to Teleoperation with clear external interface contracts (Telemetry in, Tasking in, Network Link inout). Verification strategy: measure latency at Telemetry ingress, Tasking ingress, and Network Link transmission points; correlate with operator display update and actuator command execution timestamps.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Teleoperation module is the unambiguous owner of DP4 logic. Containment score of 0.92 reflects strong cohesion: deterministic prioritization and scheduling are intrinsic teleoperation functions, not scattered across Safety Services, Vehicle, or system-level containers. The four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clean and minimal, indicating low coupling and clear responsibility boundaries. Ownership is uncontested.

diffusion_risks: Network Link bidirectional coordination: Teleoperation controls transmission scheduling discipline, but Network Link may impose its own queueing, buffering, or congestion feedback mechanisms. Risk: network-layer buffering could undermine priority isolation and introduce unpredictable latency variance. Mitigation: establish explicit contract on Network Link interface specifying (1) priority queue semantics (e.g., strict priority vs. weighted fair queueing), (2) backpressure signaling (e.g., queue depth thresholds, drop policies), (3) latency bounds per priority level. Require Network Link to expose queue state and accept Teleoperation scheduling directives.

SafetyAlert escalation latency coupling: Teleoperation must escalate safety-critical events detected during scheduling (e.g., command latency exceeds safety threshold, operator input invalid). Risk: Safety Services override or intervention could introduce secondary latency, delaying critical commands and violating SLA. Mitigation: pre-define safety override policies with explicit latency budgets; ensure SafetyAlert interface includes (1) event severity and latency metadata, (2) expected override response time, (3) fallback behavior if Safety Services does not respond within latency SLA. Document interaction in safety case.

Background traffic isolation assumption: DP4 assumes Teleoperation can isolate critical operator paths from background traffic. Risk: if Vehicle module, Telemetry module, or other subsystems generate uncontrolled background traffic on shared network or compute resources, isolation may fail and latency SLA may be violated. Mitigation: establish traffic shaping contracts with Network Link and Vehicle modules; define (1) reserved bandwidth or priority levels for teleoperation critical paths, (2) traffic policing rules (e.g., rate limits on non-critical telemetry), (3) resource reservation mechanisms (e.g., QoS tags, network slicing). Verify isolation in integration testing under worst-case background traffic load.

Scheduling algorithm diffusion risk (low): DP4 specifies 'deterministic message prioritization and scheduling' but does not detail the algorithm (e.g., rate-monotonic, deadline-monotonic, priority inheritance). Risk: if scheduling logic is implemented ad-hoc across Teleoperation module without a centralized, reusable scheduling engine, future maintenance and verification become fragmented. Mitigation: encapsulate scheduling algorithm in a cohesive sub-module or library within Teleoperation; document scheduling discipline and prove latency bounds analytically or via simulation before deployment.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module exhibits excellent containment (0.92 score) with minimal diffusion (0.08). The DP's core logic—deterministic prioritization, scheduling, and SLA enforcement—is intrinsic to teleoperation mission and naturally localized to this module. No algorithmic leakage detected. External interfaces are clean and well-defined: Telemetry and Tasking are inbound data flows; SafetyAlert and Network Link are outbound/bidirectional coordination points. The module boundary respects separation of concerns: Vehicle owns vehicle state and actuator control; Safety Services owns safety validation and override logic; Network Link owns transmission and congestion management. Teleoperation owns operator perception and command execution latency guarantees. This is a strong architectural fit.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly and sufficiently addresses FR requirement. FR demands end-to-end latency that does not degrade safe remote operation (acceptance criterion: p95 ≤ 150 ms). DP4 specifies deterministic message prioritization and scheduling layer that guarantees operator perception (sensor→display) and command execution (input→actuator) paths meet latency SLA independently of background traffic. The DP is solution-complete: it identifies the mechanism (prioritization + scheduling discipline), the scope (operator perception and command execution paths), and the constraint (SLA independence from background traffic). The DP is verifiable against the acceptance criterion via latency measurement and SLA monitoring. No logical gaps or ambiguities detected.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Network Link interface contract: Define explicit priority queue semantics, backpressure signaling, and latency bounds per priority level. Specify how Teleoperation scheduling directives are enforced by Network Link (e.g., strict priority, weighted fair queueing). Include queue state exposure and congestion feedback mechanisms.

Document SafetyAlert escalation policy: Pre-define safety override policies with explicit latency budgets and fallback behavior. Ensure SafetyAlert interface includes event severity, latency metadata, and expected response time. Integrate into safety case and verify that override latency does not violate operator SLA.

Establish traffic shaping contracts: Define reserved bandwidth, priority levels, and traffic policing rules for teleoperation critical paths. Specify rate limits on non-critical telemetry and background traffic. Verify isolation in integration testing under worst-case load scenarios.

Encapsulate scheduling algorithm: Centralize deterministic scheduling logic in a cohesive sub-module or reusable library within Teleoperation. Document scheduling discipline (e.g., rate-monotonic, deadline-monotonic) and prove latency bounds analytically or via simulation before deployment.

Define latency instrumentation and monitoring: Specify measurement points at Telemetry ingress, Tasking ingress, Network Link transmission, operator display update, and actuator command execution. Establish SLA monitoring dashboard and alert thresholds (e.g., p95 latency approaching 150 ms). Include latency variance analysis to detect scheduling anomalies.

Validate worksite safety plan integration: Ensure that Teleoperation latency SLA (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is consistent with worksite safety plan and operational policies. Document any safety-critical latency thresholds and escalation procedures. Verify that SafetyAlert escalation does not conflict with operator SLA.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The Teleoperation module exhibits excellent containment (0.92) and clear ownership boundaries. However, the diffusion risk analysis identifies four critical coupling points that require explicit sub-level decomposition to manage: (1) Network Link bidirectional coordination and priority queue semantics, (2) SafetyAlert escalation latency coupling, (3) background traffic isolation assumptions, and (4) scheduling algorithm encapsulation. These are not design flaws but rather architectural dependencies that must be formalized through sub-FR decomposition. Deriving next-level FRs will make these coupling contracts explicit, allocate responsibility for latency budgets across sub-modules, and enable verification of isolation assumptions. Safety criticality (high) mandates this rigor: latency SLA violations could compromise operator safety, and the current DP4 specification, while logically sound, defers critical interface contracts and scheduling discipline details to implementation. Sub-level decomposition will surface and resolve these deferred decisions before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Enforce deterministic message prioritization and scheduling discipline for operator perception and command execution paths, independent of background traffic and network congestion.
FR4.2: Establish and maintain latency SLA contracts with Network Link module, including priority queue semantics, backpressure signaling, and per-priority latency bounds.

FR4.3: Coordinate with Safety Services to ensure SafetyAlert escalation and override policies do not introduce secondary latency that violates operator SLA; define fallback behavior and latency budgets for safety-critical events.

FR4.4: Isolate teleoperation critical paths (sensor→display, input→actuator) from background traffic through traffic shaping, bandwidth reservation, and QoS enforcement with Vehicle and Telemetry modules.

FR4.5: Encapsulate and verify deterministic scheduling algorithm (e.g., rate-monotonic, deadline-monotonic, priority inheritance) to guarantee latency bounds analytically or via simulation before deployment.

FR4.6: Instrument and monitor end-to-end latency at Telemetry ingress, Tasking ingress, Network Link transmission, operator display update, and actuator command execution; detect and alert on SLA violations and latency variance anomalies.

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound and solution-complete for FR4, but the specification is underspecified in three critical areas that directly impact latency SLA verification and safety: (1) scheduling algorithm discipline is not named or proven (e.g., rate-monotonic vs. deadline-monotonic vs. priority inheritance); (2) Network Link interface contract is implicit and lacks formal priority queue semantics and backpressure signaling; (3) SafetyAlert escalation policy and latency budgets are not pre-defined. These are not logical gaps in the DP itself, but rather deferred design decisions that must be elevated to explicit sub-level DPs to ensure traceability and verification. Recommend revising DP4 to reference the sub-level DPs (DP4.1–DP4.6) that will formalize these contracts and algorithms. This maintains the integrity of DP4 while making its dependencies and assumptions explicit.

assumptions: Teleoperation module can enforce strict priority isolation on shared network and compute resources without interference from background traffic (Vehicle, Telemetry, other subsystems).
Network Link module will expose queue state, accept Teleoperation scheduling directives, and enforce priority queue semantics as specified in interface contract.
Safety Services will respond to SafetyAlert escalations within a pre-defined latency budget that does not violate operator SLA; fallback behavior is defined and tested.
Scheduling algorithm (rate-monotonic, deadline-monotonic, or priority inheritance) can be proven to guarantee p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under worst-case workload and network congestion.

Latency instrumentation can be deployed at all critical measurement points (Telemetry ingress, Tasking ingress, Network Link transmission, display update, actuator execution) without introducing measurement overhead that skews results.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are consistent with p95 latency SLA of 150 ms; no conflicting safety-critical latency thresholds exist.
open_risks: Network Link priority queue semantics may not align with Teleoperation scheduling discipline; bidirectional coordination could introduce unpredictable latency variance if backpressure signaling is not formalized.
SafetyAlert escalation latency coupling: if Safety Services override response time exceeds latency budget, operator SLA is violated and safety case is compromised.
Background traffic isolation may fail if Vehicle, Telemetry, or other modules generate uncontrolled traffic on shared resources; worst-case latency could exceed 150 ms p95 threshold.

Scheduling algorithm diffusion: if deterministic scheduling logic is implemented ad-hoc across Teleoperation module without centralized encapsulation, maintenance, verification, and future evolution become fragmented.

Latency instrumentation overhead: measurement points at Telemetry ingress, Tasking ingress, Network Link transmission, display update, and actuator execution could introduce timing artifacts that mask or exaggerate true latency.
Integration testing coverage: worst-case background traffic load scenarios may not be adequately represented in test plans; SLA violations may emerge in field deployment.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Network Link interface contract: priority queue semantics (strict priority vs. weighted fair queueing), backpressure signaling (queue depth thresholds, drop policies), latency bounds per priority level, queue state exposure API.

Analytical or simulation-based proof that selected scheduling algorithm (rate-monotonic, deadline-monotonic, priority inheritance) guarantees p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under worst-case workload and network congestion.
SafetyAlert escalation policy document: pre-defined override policies, latency budgets, fallback behavior, expected response time, integration into safety case.
Traffic shaping contract with Vehicle and Telemetry modules: reserved bandwidth, priority levels, traffic policing rules, rate limits on non-critical telemetry, QoS tags, network slicing mechanisms.
Latency instrumentation design: measurement point specifications, sampling strategy, overhead analysis, calibration procedures, SLA monitoring dashboard and alert thresholds.
Integration test plan: worst-case background traffic load scenarios, isolation verification under stress, latency variance analysis, SLA violation detection and logging.
Worksite safety plan alignment: confirmation that p95 latency SLA of 150 ms is consistent with operational policies and safety-critical latency thresholds; no conflicting requirements.
